title: Corner Cupboard
summary: A triangular corner hutch with mitered shelves, beadboard-lined sides, and a choice of inset or overlay doors.
description:
The whole cabinet self-corrects for lumber variation, and that's the first real skill in the build: measure the actual width of your 1x8 back board (it can genuinely run anywhere from 7" to 7-1/2" depending on the mill), then cut each 1x12 shelf's mitered corner to match that measured width rather than a number off a plan. Cut one end of the 1x12 at 45°, measure your actual 1x8 width, then make the second 45° cut perpendicular to the first — repeat that for all six shelves and every one self-adjusts to your real lumber.

Beadboard on the front sides is doing double duty structurally, not just visually: the source is explicit that the front trim placement doesn't need to be perfect, because the beadboard is strong enough to cover for it. That's worth knowing before agonizing over trim alignment that the next step is going to hide anyway.

cutList.note: Measure your actual board width — it varies by mill run.
Both ends cut at 45°, the second cut perpendicular to the first and sized to your actual back-board width.
Sized to your actual door openings — see the doors step.

steps.title: Measure and cut the shelves
steps.body: Measure the actual width of your 1x8 back board first — it can run anywhere from 7" to 7-1/2" depending on the mill, and every shelf is sized off it. Cut one end of a 1x12 shelf blank at 45°, measure your real back-board width along the short edge, and make the second 45° cut perpendicular to the first. That gives a trapezoid whose long front edge runs exactly 22-1/2" longer than the back edge, because each 45° cut across an 11-1/4" board runs out 11-1/4"; the cut list's 30-1/2" corresponds to a back board right around 8", so compute your own figure rather than cutting to 30-1/2" blind. Repeat for all six shelves, and cut them alternating — flip the board over between shelves so each one nests into the offcut the last one left. Nesting is what makes two 6 ft 1x12s go far enough; cut them as separate rectangles and you will run out.

steps.title: Cut the back and the trim
steps.body: Cut the 1x8 back to 72", the same length as the two 1x3 front side trim pieces, so the cabinet's three vertical members all finish flush top and bottom. Cut those two front side trim pieces at 72" as well — note that each one uses a whole 8 ft 1x3 and leaves under 24", so the four 30" door frame pieces will not come out of the offcuts; buy a fourth 1x3 rather than discovering this at the doors step. Cut the top and bottom trim to 26-1/2" from the 1x4 and the tabletop trim to 26-1/2" from the 1x2, but treat all three as rough lengths and trim them to the assembled cabinet later, as the cut list's own notes say. Leave the beadboard, the crown moulding and the door parts uncut until their steps — every one of them is measured off the cabinet as built.

steps.title: Attach the shelves to the back
steps.body: Mark the back board for all shelf locations — every shelf here is fixed, not adjustable. Predrill or pocket-hole each joint, apply glue, and attach the shelves to the back with 2" screws.

steps.title: Add the front side trim
steps.body: Mark the front side trim 1/2" in from the back edge along its length, then mark all shelf locations from the previous step. Predrill and attach with 2" screws on the inside edge and 1-1/4" screws on the outer edge, two screws per shelf, with glue. This trim doesn't need to be perfect — the beadboard in the next step covers it.

steps.title: Add the beadboard
steps.body: Measure the open spaces on the back sides and cut the beadboard to fit, aiming for a tight fit. Apply glue to the exposed shelf edges and attach the beadboard with 1-1/4" screws, keeping it flush to the back board — that seam is the one that will show most.

steps.title: Add the top and bottom trim
steps.body: Measure the front of the assembled cabinet rather than using the 26-1/2" from the cut list, since the finished front width depends on the back board you actually measured back in the first step. Cut the 1x4 top and bottom trim and the 1x2 tabletop trim to those measurements, dry-fit each before fastening, and attach with glue and 1-1/4" screws into the shelf edges and the front side trim behind them. Keep the top edge of the tabletop trim flush with the shelf it caps so the surface stays flat for anything set on it.

steps.title: Add the top moulding
steps.body: Crown on a corner cabinet meets at outside corners, so measure the top of the cabinet and cut the mitered returns from the 36" length of moulding — cut one, dry-fit it, then cut its partner, because a crown miter that is a degree off shows badly at eye level. Cut every piece long and creep up on the fit. Fasten with brad nails into the trim behind it and glue the miters themselves, which is what keeps the joint closed as the wood moves.

steps.title: Build the doors
steps.body: Measure your actual door opening first and size the 1x3 frame parts to it — the cut list's four 30" pieces are a starting length, not a finished one, and they will need a fourth 1x3 board since the two 72" trim pieces already took two. Build the overlay frame flat with a pocket hole jig, or with 2" wood screws through the joints, checking both diagonals as you clamp. Attach beadboard to the BACK of the assembled frame, sized to the opening in the shelf behind it. An inset door works too if you have a router or table saw to rabbet the frame, but keep the whole door under 3/4" thick — the shelf sits directly behind it and a thicker door will not close.

steps.title: Add the center post and hang the doors
steps.body: Fit a center post between the two doors from 1x3 offcut to close the gap where they meet and give the doors something to shut against. Hang each door on its pair of hinges, mortising the hinge leaves flush if you want the doors to sit tight, and adjust until the gap around and between them is even — hang the first door, get it right, then set the second to match it rather than to the cabinet. Sand every surface, fill any screw holes, and finish.

images.alt: Triangular corner cupboard with beadboard sides
